sentence: Although the Director General will act independently, Ministers will continue to have a wide range of responsibility under the Bill, and it is not my intention or wish to see any impairment of the present freedom to question Ministers about competition or consumer protection matters.
reason: The sentence explicitly refers to preserving "freedom to question Ministers," which is about maintaining the absence of restrictions or barriers to parliamentary scrutiny and accountability.

sentence: for, in this land of freedom and equal rights, the law was "no respecter of persons"—all men were alike entitled to its fostering protection.
reason: The sentence emphasizes law's equal protection of all citizens from interference regardless of their status, which is fundamentally about protection from arbitrary treatment rather than empowerment to act.

sentence: Who are these people who have had their freedom of speech removed from them?
reason: This refers to freedom of speech being "removed," which is about external interference with the right to speak freely - a classic example of negative liberty.

sentence: I will not discuss the matter as one of history, or go through all the instances in which armies have proved themselves either the friends or the enemies of freedom.
reason: The sentence explicitly presents armies as both potential protectors and destroyers of freedom, making it impossible to classify as purely positive or negative liberty since it acknowledges both the enabling and constraining aspects of military force on liberty.

sentence: That is one of the main reasons, alongside oil, why we have to maintain freedom of navigation.
reason: This refers to freedom from interference with naval passage and shipping routes, which is about preventing external obstruction rather than enabling capacity to act.

sentence: Whiteside) would tell the Roman Catholic gentlemen of these kingdoms that throughout the Continent, and more especially throughout Italy, the great mass of the educated Roman Catholic laity regarded the  Index Expurgatorius  as a frightful instrument for crushing the freedom of the human mind;
reason: The Index Expurgatorius is described as an instrument that "crushes" freedom of the mind, representing external censorship and interference with intellectual freedom, which is a classic example of restricting negative liberty.

sentence: There can be no doubt that he has re-established the Queen's Law in Ireland, and has given back liberty to the Queen's subjects, that he has administered the laws with firmness and fairness to all classes.
reason: The reference to re-establishing law and giving back liberty to subjects emphasizes freedom from lawlessness and disorder, restoring protection from interference rather than empowering new capacities.

sentence: As the representative of one of the largest, if not the largest, constituencies in England, covering an area of more than 1,000 square miles, I have always opposed this unnecessary and idiotic restriction on the freedom of the subject.
reason: The speaker opposes a "restriction on the freedom of the subject," which is explicitly about removing external constraints or interference imposed on individuals.

sentence: Bright signifying the contrary—that if the supremacy of Parliament were preserved, there was no limit that I would fix in granting local liberty to Ireland.
reason: The phrase "granting local liberty to Ireland" refers to empowering Ireland with capacity for self-governance and local decision-making authority, which is about enabling action rather than removing interference.

sentence: NCAT reports represent a key step in any hospital reconfiguration and need to be properly scrutinised, so on 17 July I requested a copy of the report under the Freedom of Information Act.
reason: The Freedom of Information Act embodies negative liberty by preventing government interference with citizens' access to public information - it's freedom FROM government secrecy or obstruction of information access.

sentence: and upon another covering the continental shelf under which the waters above the shelf outside the territorial sea are recognised as being high seas in which there is freedom to fish.
reason: This describes freedom from interference in fishing activities in international waters, which is a classic negative liberty - the absence of restrictions on an activity.

sentence: The House of Commons was at liberty to restrict its own privileges;
reason: This refers to the House's freedom from external constraints in determining its own privileges, which is about absence of interference in self-governance.

sentence: and I maintain that no knot or section of the people in a corner of the land has the right to obstruct the path to peace and freedom of the masses of the nation.
reason: The sentence focuses on preventing obstruction of "freedom of the masses," emphasizing the removal of external interference from "a knot or section of the people" who would block others' liberty.

sentence: to make further provision with regard to the health, local government and improvement of the city, the regulation of commons in and near the city, and with regard to the freedom of the city;
reason: This phrase refers to "freedom of the city," which is a ceremonial honor or civic privilege granted by a municipality, not a concept relating to either positive or negative liberty in Berlin's framework.

sentence: On top of all this is the number of restrictions on the railways' freedom in the matter of charges, dating from the days when, having swallowed the canals, they were really a monopoly.
reason: The sentence refers to restrictions on the railways' freedom to set charges, which is about removing external constraints on their ability to act as they choose - a classic case of freedom from interference.

sentence: When the present Opposition talks about the freedom of the Press, it is the freedom of the big business concerns run by the combines and big capital interests to control the Press in the interests of a small clique of people who have hitherto controlled and owned the industries and wealth of this country.
reason: The speaker is critiquing how "freedom of the Press" is being invoked to protect press owners from interference or regulation, which is fundamentally about freedom from external constraints, even though the speaker views this negatively.

sentence: There is no Member of this House so deeply attached to freedom of debate as I am.
reason: This refers to freedom from restrictions on parliamentary debate - the absence of interference with the right to speak freely in Parliament.

sentence: With this view he had taken the liberty to second the motion of his hon.
reason: This uses "liberty" in the procedural sense of parliamentary etiquette, meaning the speaker had taken the privilege or permission to second a motion, not referring to either freedom from interference or capacity for action.

sentence: A satisfactory solution of the question can only be guaranteed by the grant of liberty to the Government of India to devise those tariff arrangements which seem best fitted to India's needs as an integral portion of the British Empire….
reason: The sentence advocates for granting India the capacity and empowerment to actively devise and shape its own tariff arrangements, which is about enabling action rather than merely removing restrictions.

sentence: The Government are failing to ensure freedom for women to lead their own lives and travel in security.
reason: This refers to freedom from interference with women's ability to travel and live without threats to their security, focusing on removing external barriers and ensuring safety from harm.

sentence: Thomas William Grady was at perfect liberty to relinquish the said annuity, and separate his political interest in the county of Limerick from Mr.
reason: The phrase describes freedom from external constraint - Grady was not being coerced or prevented from relinquishing the annuity and separating his political interests.

sentence: I suggest that the true interpretation of the present situation is that there are two parties quarrelling in Montenegro, and that the Serbian intervention is, on the whole, for the diplomatic purpose of trying to get peace between them and to protect the freedom of opinion in Montenegro from foreign intervention.
reason: The phrase "protect the freedom of opinion in Montenegro from foreign intervention" explicitly refers to preventing external interference in Montenegro's expression of views, which is a classic example of negative liberty.

sentence: He had on a former occasion shown, that during the administration of sir Robert Walpole there were 118 more placemen in the House of Commons than at present, and yet public liberty was more secure at that time than it was now.
reason: The reference to "public liberty" being "more secure" in the past context of discussing government placemen (officials) in Parliament suggests freedom from government interference or corruption, which is a negative liberty concept.

sentence: My lords, the noble lord who now presides upon the woolsack, has long been the friend of liberty and the constitution;
reason: In the historical parliamentary context, being a "friend of liberty and the constitution" refers to defending traditional constitutional freedoms and rights against government overreach or tyranny, which is fundamentally about negative liberty.

sentence: The liberty of the subject was infringed by the lengthened period of incarceration before trial, and an injustice was done from Judges not going constantly into the country and doing the work which the public had a right to expect from them.
reason: The sentence focuses on freedom from prolonged detention without trial and the right to timely justice, which are protections against state interference with individual liberty.

sentence: Baronet charged those who voted in the minority with having adopted that course from motives which they repudiated, he would take the liberty of telling that right hon.
reason: This use of "liberty" is a procedural parliamentary phrase meaning "permission to speak/respond," not referring to either concept of freedom.

sentence: and, in the cause, in which it has been displayed, we lament to see nothing characteristic of that love of freedom, for which this nation has heretofore been so highly renowned in the world.
reason: The phrase refers to Britain's traditional "love of freedom" as a national characteristic, which in historical context typically meant freedom from tyranny, oppression, and external control - the classic negative liberty concept of being free from interference.

sentence: If there had been encouragement and liberty given to private enterprise to make a start now, I am sure a proposal of that kind would meet with the approval of a large number of Members of this House.
reason: The sentence advocates for removing restrictions on private enterprise ("liberty given to private enterprise"), which is about freedom from government interference in business activities.

sentence: I hope that in the near future we shall have a Bill covering a larger and wider range and dealing fairly with all those sections of very important people, important because they affect very much the every-day life and liberty of our people.
reason: The phrase "every-day life and liberty" in context of needing a Bill to protect "important people" who "affect" citizens' liberty suggests concern about protecting people from interference or coercion by these influential groups.

sentence: We attach high priority to making a full reality of the freedom to provide services throughout the European Community.
reason: This refers to enabling and empowering businesses to actively provide services across borders, which is about creating capacity and opportunity to act rather than removing restrictions.

sentence:
The largest liberty was
 
 273 
intended, and he believed was given, by the Bill with respect to improving and amending anything that the University or Commissioners might do in defining the qualifications of members of private halls.
reason: This refers to the liberty to actively improve and amend regulations, which is about empowerment to take constructive action rather than freedom from interference.

sentence: The third course, and the only logical course, if we are to expect consistency at all, is to be found in the pursuance of the liberal spirit which is supposed to inspire the reforms of last year, and which we were told this afternoon aims at leading the people of India into liberty.
reason: The sentence describes "leading the people of India into liberty" which indicates empowerment and developing capacity for self-governance, characteristic of positive liberty rather than merely removing constraints.

sentence: Gentleman should be in no doubt that the Government are in the forefront of those criticising Nigeria for its continued abuse of the rights of its own people, arbitrary detentions, repression of basic freedoms and erosion of democracy.
reason: This sentence focuses on freedoms being violated through "abuse of rights," "arbitrary detentions," and "repression of basic freedoms" - all describing external interference and coercion by the Nigerian government against its citizens.

sentence: He would beg the House to remark, that in the first year of freedom the deficiency was only one-tenth.
reason: The context of "first year of freedom" refers to the absence of slavery or bondage, which is fundamentally about removing external coercion and interference rather than empowerment to act.

sentence:
In this, the greatest of all struggles for human liberty, in this great


hour of world history, are we to contemplate at the end of it the continuance of racial hatred and of nationalist, imperialist and capitalist ambitions?
reason: The phrase "struggles for human liberty" in the context of 1939 (WWII beginning) refers to the fight against fascist oppression and tyranny - a struggle for freedom FROM external domination, racial persecution, and imperialist control.

sentence: As a proud Conservative, I fundamentally believe in individual freedoms, and I believe that current divorce laws inhibit that freedom in the broad, vague name of keeping families together.
reason: The speaker frames divorce laws as external constraints that "inhibit" individual freedom, advocating for freedom from legal interference in personal decisions about ending marriages.

sentence: But all that proved that freedom of conscience was a great advantage to a country, and that a Government which protected freedom of conscience protected at the same time the development of individual skill and industry.
reason: The sentence focuses on freedom of conscience as something to be "protected" by government from interference, which is the classic negative liberty conception of religious freedom as freedom from state coercion in matters of belief.

sentence: He would put it to every gentleman in the House—to those, even, who cheered the noble lord's speech—whether, on their conscience, they did not believe that the objects of the noble lord were, on the one hand, to put down the liberty of the press, and, on the other, to prevent all public meetings.
reason: The sentence explicitly refers to preventing government interference with press freedom and public assembly, which are classic examples of freedom from external coercion or censorship.

sentence: At the same time, he wished, in case any objection should be made, it might be understood he was at liberty to offer some observations to their lordships upon the subject.
reason: This refers to procedural liberty in parliamentary debate - the speaker's right to make observations to the House, which is a formal parliamentary procedure rather than a philosophical concept of liberty.

sentence: that that occasioned a dissertation on the Liberty of the Press;
reason: This refers to freedom of the press, which is a classic negative liberty concerning freedom from government censorship or interference with publication.

sentence: There never was a purer fiction for a greater effort of the imagination than to describe as a "war of freedom" the movement of the fanatical, savage, cruel, bloodthirsty, and barbarous chieftain.
reason: This sentence uses "freedom" rhetorically to criticize the characterization of a war rather than discussing liberty as a political concept of individual or collective rights.

sentence: but, if they chose to regard it as a vote of want of confidence in the self-constituted tribunal called the Committee of Council on Education, they were at liberty to take it so.
reason: This refers to freedom from interference in how MPs choose to interpret a vote, emphasizing their right to hold their own view without external constraint on their judgment.

sentence: Is it resulting in the re-establishment of law and order in Greece, in the re-establishment of the peace and tranquility of civil liberty and the liberty of the subject?
reason: The phrase refers to "civil liberty and the liberty of the subject" in the context of law and order, which concerns freedom from arbitrary interference and protection of individual rights against coercion by the state or others.

sentence: " That assertion, however, he would take the liberty of denying.
reason: This use of "liberty" is a procedural/formal expression meaning "permission to do something" (in this case, to deny an assertion), not a philosophical concept of freedom.

sentence: It was sanctified, not only by the passionate desire of Munster, Connaught, and Leinster to stand with Ireland, but by the fact that some of the most superb, the most glorious, and the most courageous fights for liberty against the domination of this country were organised by gallant Protestants whose names stand on the beadroll of Irish glory.
reason: The sentence describes historical fights "for liberty against the domination of this country," which refers to freedom from external control and interference by Britain over Ireland.

sentence: It was those refugees to freedom who really made possible the scientific advance that took place.
reason: The term "refugees to freedom" refers to people fleeing from oppression or persecution, emphasizing freedom as escape from external coercion rather than empowerment to act.

sentence: One is to make certain that when a territory gains its freedom it should understand how dangerous it is to throw away a skilled Civil Service, especially a technical Civil Service, which is available to it.
reason: The sentence discusses gaining freedom as an act of empowerment and self-governance (independence), with the capacity to make choices about retaining civil service structures, rather than freedom from external interference.

sentence:
The conduct of the Government in thus in-
 
 1145 
terfering with the liberty of the people was most infamous.
reason: This refers to government interference restricting people's freedom from external coercion, which is the core of negative liberty.

sentence: Everybody was at liberty, if he pleased, to change his surname, but no one else was obliged to recognise the change unless he pleased.
reason: This refers to the absence of external restrictions on one's ability to change their surname - there is no law or coercion preventing this action, which is a classic example of freedom from interference.

sentence: What more natural than that the people of Ireland, "rightly struggling to be free," should be with the Boers, who also are rightly struggling to maintain their freedom and to keep you out of the Transvaal?
reason: This refers to freedom from external domination and interference - both the Irish struggle against British rule and the Boers' struggle to keep the British out of the Transvaal are about maintaining independence from outside control.

sentence: Unless tobacco is made illegal, commercial freedom of speech should not be sacrificed in terms of banning tobacco advertising.
reason: This sentence defends freedom from government interference in commercial speech, specifically opposing censorship/banning of tobacco advertising.

sentence: People who understand freedom know that if we want peace we must prepare for war — high-intensity war.
reason: The statement frames freedom as requiring protection from external threats through military preparedness, emphasizing defense against interference rather than empowerment to act.

sentence: Freedom of religion and freedom of speech are vital and interconnected rights.
reason: Both freedoms mentioned (religion and speech) are classic negative liberties that protect individuals from external interference or censorship by the state or others.

sentence: My question was whether merchants were at liberty to accept consignments of wheat and barley for this country, and whether permits would be given for the importation of this necessary material, or whether it was the intention of the Government to divert this trade to neutral countries.
reason: The question concerns whether merchants are free from government restrictions on accepting and importing wheat and barley, focusing on the absence of regulatory barriers to trade.

sentence: Lady is right: the end of freedom of movement has inevitably had some consequences for touring artists.
reason: This refers to freedom of movement as an absence of restrictions on travel and work across borders, which touring artists have lost due to new barriers and constraints.

sentence: That is the spirit in which I propose to take the liberty of addressing the House on this occasion, and I propose to bring forward as temperately and as moderately as I can one or two points which I think the House ought to consider and bear in mind before coming to a decision on the Second Reading of this Bill.
reason: "Take the liberty of" is a procedural phrase meaning "take the opportunity to" or "presume to," not referring to either positive or negative concepts of freedom.

sentence: Now, Sir, having thus stated what the distress among the clergy of Ireland is, what the system of opposition to the tithe is, and what is the general result of the whole facts of the case, I will take the liberty of reading to the Committee the first Resolution which I have to propose.
reason: This use of "liberty" is procedural, meaning the speaker is requesting permission or asserting their right to perform a parliamentary action (reading a resolution), rather than discussing concepts of freedom or empowerment.

sentence: It is not possible to combine the inexorable march of Socialism with the preservation of personal freedom.
reason: This sentence frames personal freedom as something to be "preserved" from the external interference of socialism's "inexorable march," emphasizing freedom from state coercion rather than empowerment to act.

sentence: Gentleman advises us to carry the line of conquest by British and Christian arms among the Mahommedan people struggling for their liberty in the Soudan.
reason: The sentence refers to people "struggling for their liberty" against conquest by external forces, indicating freedom from foreign domination and interference.

sentence: Workers, both men and women, who were transferred by, or with the approval of, my Department to employment away from home on or after 1st June, 1940, are eligible for free railway warrants to enable them to return home when they are at liberty to leave their present employment.
reason: "At liberty" here is used in a procedural sense meaning "when they are permitted/able to leave" their employment, not referring to either freedom from interference or empowerment to act.

sentence: That is the point that I was seeking to make — that, even if there is this marvellous freedom of choice whether to increase taxes or cut public expenditure, there are constraints on taxation.
reason: The sentence discusses "constraints on taxation" as external limitations that restrict the government's freedom to tax, which is fundamentally about freedom from interference or restriction rather than empowerment to act.

sentence: The Society of Editors, the National Union of Journalists, with the backing of the TUC, and many others concerned with the freedom of the press, have said that there is the potential to ride roughshod over freedoms.
reason: "Freedom of the press" refers to freedom from government interference or censorship of journalism, which is a classic negative liberty concerning absence of external constraints on expression.

sentence: That is a counter-blast to the statements made recently by Representative Claire Luce, who said that the freedom of the seas failed to prevent the first and second world wars and there was no reason to think that the freedom of the air would prevent a third world war.
reason: "Freedom of the seas" refers to the absence of restrictions on maritime navigation and commerce, which is a classic example of freedom from external interference or control.

sentence: I refuse to admit that there is any reflection on the Chair when it is proposed that there should be some security for the liberty of debate.
reason: The phrase "liberty of debate" refers to freedom from restrictions or interference with parliamentary discussion, which is a classic example of negative liberty as it concerns the absence of external constraints on speech.

sentence: My noble friend was eloquent on the question of the liberty of the subject.
reason: This refers to traditional civil liberties and individual rights against state interference, which is the classic domain of negative liberty.

sentence:
The objection that compulsion ought not to be enforced where there was only one school, which might be a denominational school that was distasteful to the parents,
 
 1925 
was a most unreasonable objection, for there was the most perfect freedom of withdrawal from religious instruction under the Conscience Clause, which certainly was valueless, though not in the sense suggested, but only because parents had no desire to avail themselves of it.

sentence: We on this side also believe in liberty and in freedom, and it is because we believe in liberty and freedom that we are opposing this Bill.
reason: The speaker opposes the Bill to prevent government interference with liberty, indicating a concern for freedom from external constraints rather than empowerment to act.

sentence:
Huskisson described his measure:—
 "I am prepared to open the commerce of our colonies to all friendly states, upon the same principle (though of course with some difference in the detail of its modifications) upon which they are at liberty to trade with Jersey or with Ireland.
reason: Huskisson is describing the removal of trade restrictions, allowing friendly states to trade with colonies without external interference, which is freedom from commercial barriers.

sentence: Surely they will see that this proposal involves the grossest interference with the liberty of the subject, and is about the most tyrannous proposal that ever was submitted to this House.
reason: The sentence explicitly describes "interference with the liberty of the subject" and characterizes it as "tyrannous," which clearly refers to freedom from external coercion or interference by authorities.

sentence: Chamberlain) thought it could not be maintained in face of the fact that freedom of contract had already been interfered with by the Legislature when the interest of any particular class seemed to demand it.
reason: This refers to legislative interference with freedom of contract, which is fundamentally about external constraints on individuals' ability to make agreements without government restriction.

sentence: I have already mentioned immigration, and we know that ending freedom of movement is the big prize in this country.
reason: This refers to ending freedom of movement as a "prize," implying the restriction of people's ability to move freely across borders is desirable - this is about removing an external freedom/right to movement.

sentence: Unity now on the eve of the great battle against the grand foe of liberty, Nonconformity, and Wales—the House of Lords.
reason: The "grand foe of liberty" refers to the House of Lords as an institution that interferes with or blocks freedoms, particularly those of Nonconformists and Wales, framing liberty as freedom from external oppression or control.

sentence: and he approved warmly what the Prime Minister had said last night—that he was not willing to war against people who were fighting for their freedom.
reason: The reference to "fighting for their freedom" indicates resistance against external domination or oppression, which is fundamentally about freedom from interference rather than empowerment to act.

sentence: A difficulty of another kind arose in cases where slaves existed on estates entailed or mortgaged, and where, of course, the possessor had not the power to agree to their claims for purchasing their freedom.
reason: This refers to slaves' inability to purchase their freedom due to external legal constraints (entailment/mortgage), which is about removing obstacles to their liberty rather than empowering them with new capacities.

sentence: The Liberal Democrats would separate capital and revenue spending and give local authorities the freedom to invest the money they have in new homes.
reason: This describes empowering local authorities with the capacity to invest and build new homes, focusing on enabling action rather than removing restrictions.

sentence: and, as I consider, in a manner disastrously detrimental to the interests of private Members, and of freedom inside and outside the House.
reason: The phrase refers to "freedom inside and outside the House" being harmed by detrimental interference, indicating a concern about restrictions or constraints on MPs' ability to speak and act without external coercion.

sentence: Is there no way in which this happiness and freedom from fear can be combined with the interests of the Polish Army.
reason: "Freedom from fear" explicitly refers to the absence of fear or threat, which is a classic example of negative liberty - freedom from a negative condition or interference.

sentence: The Government have failed to give local authorities the freedom to raise funding by going to the market for, for example, bond finance.
reason: This refers to empowering local authorities with the capacity to act (raising funds through bond finance), not freedom from interference.

sentence: As the Government continue to impose further unprecedented restrictions on people’s freedoms, it is important to give people hope and justification.
reason: The sentence refers to "restrictions on people's freedoms" as external constraints being imposed by the government, which is the classic formulation of negative liberty as freedom from interference.

sentence: but he thought that the petitioners acted up to the true spirit and letter of christian charity, when they came forward and endeavoured to maintain pure religion— when they endeavoured to support Protestantism and the principles of civil and religious liberty against Popish domination —when they endeavoured to maintain the church of England against the church of Rome.
reason: The phrase "civil and religious liberty against Popish domination" clearly frames liberty as freedom from external religious interference and coercion, specifically from Catholic ("Popish") control over Protestant religious and civil practices.

sentence: The impression that I have formed, and I am not afraid to say it, is that instead of food, we have forms, instead of foundations for our houses we have forms, instead of freedom we have forms, and instead of fuel we have forms.
reason: The speaker is criticizing bureaucratic forms as obstacles that prevent access to basic necessities and freedom, emphasizing interference with what people should be free FROM rather than what they should be empowered TO do.

sentence: He is the great champion of the cause of freedom when he is Leader of the Opposition, but not so keen about it when he is a Minister of the Crown.
reason: This refers to freedom in the general political sense of opposing government restrictions and interference, which aligns with negative liberty as freedom from state control.

sentence: Labour and liberty.

sentence: He told the Committee that Major Lushington, turning to the proctors who were in court, said, "Gentlemen, any of you are at liberty to cross-examine the witnesses, and to give the prisoner any assistance in his present circumstances.

sentence: Under such circumstances, I felt peculiarly disposed, in common with many others, not only to support his Majesty's government in the adoption of any measures which they might think the situation of affairs required, but scrupulously to abstain from all discussion and enquiry that might embarrass them, at a moment when freedom of action appeared so desirable to the public cause [Sir James seemed here to be much affected by indisposition.
reason: The phrase "freedom of action" refers to the government's need to be free from interference or obstruction (the speaker abstaining from "discussion and enquiry that might embarrass them"), which is freedom from external constraints rather than empowerment to act.

sentence: I would appeal to those who will be merged from the borough forces to use this legislation, but I do hope that to them, and to all police, there will be granted at some date hot far distant that liberty which hitherto the county borough police have had, of watch committee or standing joint committee consideration accompanied by right of appeal to the Home Secretary.

sentence: Commercial freedom forsooth!

sentence: Friend was correct, but he would remind him that the absence of penal statutes did not alone constitute religious liberty;
reason: The speaker is discussing "religious liberty" in terms of the removal of penal statutes (laws that punish), which is about freedom from state interference and punishment in religious matters.

sentence: He must again express his regret at hearing that there was to be no representative system in the colony until slavery was removed, and the population so condensed as that their representatives might come together without much inconvenience, which was putting off freedom for ever.
reason: The speaker discusses the denial of a representative system and political participation, which concerns the capacity for self-governance and democratic empowerment, not merely freedom from interference.

sentence: If the law contravened natural right, and outraged the principles of civil and religious liberty, a constitutional resistance to it was a duty.
reason: The sentence focuses on resistance to law that "outraged the principles of civil and religious liberty," emphasizing freedom from government interference in civil and religious matters rather than empowerment to act.

sentence: We are now examining questions of the liberty of Irish citizens who come into Northern Ireland, who may not even know that they are in Northern Ireland.

sentence: I, for my part, certainly hope we will have an opportunity to test the will of the House on the regulations, because this is the first freedom we have been given on such regulations for months.
reason: The speaker is referring to freedom from government restriction of parliamentary procedures - being free from interference that prevented them from voting on regulations.

sentence: but, under this law, life could not be affected, although liberty might be somewhat abridged to secure geral tranquillity.

sentence: Member and I will say to those Governments, "The odium you are attracting in the Third World, among the uncommitted nations, in those countries which are confused about where real peace, justice and freedom lies, will so redound to your discredit that in your own vested interests you will be wiser to behave differently".

Gentleman, and I can only arrive at one conclusion—namely, that they point to the abolition of that religious settlement which has prevailed in this

sentence: The sense of despair that comes from the deprivation of liberty was evident.

sentence: I believe these benefits in the main outweigh the limitations which it imposes on our freedom to cut back imports more drastically.

sentence: He quite admitted there were still injustices and wrongs in Ireland to be set right, and that we had not yet attained to anything approaching absolute civil and religious liberty in Ireland;

sentence: I hope a new phase of things is arising where the same freedom and liberty of action will be accorded to the agricultural labourer as are enjoyed by other sections of the industrial world.

sentence: Boards of guardians have every freedom in that respect.
